eras.name: Indigenous Amazigh Foundations
eras.description: Characterized by practical, heavy wool garments designed for the Atlas Mountains. Fashion was deeply symbolic, utilizing tribal tattoos and intricate silver jewelry to denote status and lineage.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A high-detail historical illustration of an 11th-century Amazigh woman in the Atlas Mountains, wearing a heavy hand-woven white wool Haik secured by silver triangular fibula brooches, featuring intricate geometric facial tattoos and layered amber bead necklaces.

eras.name: Andalusian Refinement
eras.description: The influx of refugees from Al-Andalus brought sophisticated urban tailoring and silk weaving to cities like Fes and Tetouan. This era saw the introduction of complex embroidery and the early evolution of the structured Caftan.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 17th-century Moroccan noble in a Fes courtyard, wearing a layered silk Caftan with gold thread embroidery (Sfifa) and a traditional red felt Tarboosh hat, cinematic lighting, Moorish architecture background.

eras.name: French & Spanish Protectorate Era
eras.description: A period of stylistic dualism where Western silhouettes began to influence urban elites. Traditional garments like the Djellaba were modernized with sharper tailoring while maintaining their cultural essence.
eras.aiImagePrompt: 1940s Casablanca street scene, a man wearing a tailored wool Djellaba over a French-style collared shirt and trousers, leather Babouche, blending colonial and traditional aesthetics.

eras.name: Post-Independence Renaissance
eras.description: A resurgence of national pride led to the elevation of the Caftan into a high-fashion garment. Designers began experimenting with lighter fabrics and vibrant colors for the international jet-set visiting Marrakech.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1970s fashion editorial style shot of a woman in Marrakech wearing a two-piece Takchita in emerald green velvet with heavy gold embroidery and a wide metallic Mdamma belt, poolside setting.

eras.name: Contemporary Caftan Couture
eras.description: Led by designers like Albert Oiknine, the Moroccan Caftan entered the global luxury stage. The focus shifted to 'Haute Couture' techniques, incorporating Swarovski crystals and unconventional cuts.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A modern runway shot of an Albert Oiknine inspired luxury Caftan, sheer silk organza layers, intricate silver filigree hand-embroidery, high-fashion lighting, dramatic silhouette.

eras.name: Neo-Moroccan Streetwear
eras.description: A youth-led movement blending traditional motifs with global streetwear. This era is defined by oversized silhouettes, Arabic calligraphy prints, and the repurposing of traditional fabrics like Sfifa on hoodies.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A young Moroccan man in the Casablanca medina wearing an oversized black hoodie with gold Sfifa trim along the sleeves and Arabic typography, mixing high-street fashion with heritage craft.

textiles.name: Sabra Silk
textiles.description: Known as 'vegetable silk,' it is derived from the Agave cactus and features a distinct metallic sheen and heavy drape.

textiles.name: Fes Embroidery (Terz Fassi)
textiles.description: Intricate, reversible geometric patterns usually in blue or green on white cotton or linen.

textiles.name: Moroccan Leather
textiles.description: World-renowned soft goatskin and sheepskin, traditionally processed in ancient tanneries.

textiles.name: Sfifa
textiles.description: A decorative woven braid used to finish the edges of traditional garments, often made with gold or silver threads.

climate.fashionImplication: The extreme heat of the interior necessitates loose-fitting, breathable natural fibers like linen and cotton, while the cool Atlas nights require heavy wool wraps.
